does: This paper introduces Superintelligent Retrieval Agent (SIRA), a system that transforms multi-round exploratory retrieval into a single, highly efficient retrieval action. The core innovation treats retrieval as a corpus-discriminative problem rather than a simple relevance-matching task. SIRA operates in two phases: offline, it enriches documents with supplementary search vocabulary via language model augmentation; at query time, it predicts vocabulary the input query lacks and uses corpus statistics as filtering tools to eliminate terms that are absent, overly common, or unlikely to create discriminative signal. The final retrieval combines these signals into a weighted BM25 call, eliminating the iterative query-reformulation loop typical of exploratory search while improving recall and reducing latency.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of retrieval-augmented generation and agentic retrieval systems that treat knowledge base access as a fundamental capability of large language models. It responds to limitations in existing approaches that perform naive iterative search, where agents reformulate queries reactively based on snippet inspection. The paper draws from corpus-discriminative ranking literature and vocabulary enrichment techniques, adapting insights about term importance and retrieval margin from classical information retrieval. It extends prior work on LLM-enhanced document representations and builds on corpus statistics as a filtering mechanism, combining these threads to move beyond black-box retrieval toward more expert-like search behavior.
enables: SIRA points toward more efficient and scalable retrieval-augmented systems that could reduce the computational overhead of multi-round agentic loops. By compressing exploration into a single discriminative retrieval call, it enables deployment scenarios where latency is critical and token budgets are tight. The approach opens directions for learning retrieval vocabularies end-to-end, applying similar discriminative principles to dense retrievers, and extending corpus-aware rewriting to other domains beyond text search. It also suggests broader agentic improvements where expensive reasoning steps are compressed via offline preparation, potentially influencing how agents interact with external tools and knowledge bases in production systems.
